cataloging_subject.label: Iacob and Iosep
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse retelling of the Jacob and Joseph narrative, extant as 538 lines plus a Latin explicit in Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652, fols. 1r–10v. This abstract work is distinct from that composite manuscript, Arthur S. Napier's 1916 edition and its later reprint, the University of Michigan OCR and page images, the repository XML artifact, and a possibly related but unresolved fragment on fol. 3r of Beinecke Takamiya MS 32.

catalog_summary.title: Iacob and Iosep
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably second half of the thirteenth century; one corpus proposes 1260–1290
catalog_summary.region: England; original dialect and place of composition unresolved

identifiers.scheme: Digital Index of Middle English Verse
New Index of Middle English Verse

notes: The wording is preserved in the manuscript explicit; Napier supplied the same wording as a bracketed heading because the manuscript has no opening title.

titles.value: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century
notes: This is an edition-layer title; its thirteenth-century dating is not an original medieval subtitle.

titles.value: Wolle ȝe nou ihere wordes swiþe gode / Of one patriarke after Noees flode?

scope.description: Brief Latin closing formula following the Middle English poem

notes: The formula is too brief to make the whole work multilingual; discovery language remains Middle English.

agents.name: Arthur S. Napier
notes: 1853–1916; editor of the immediate source edition, not author of the medieval poem.
Modern proposals that the poet was a minstrel, a clerically trained poet, or a household chaplain are interpretive and remain unresolved.

scope.description: Vernacular verse reshaping of the Jacob and Joseph narrative in Genesis 37–47 and related medieval biblical traditions
Transcription of Bodley 652, fols. 1r–10v, with readings and emendations in footnotes

date_statements.display: Probably second half of the thirteenth century
notes: The date is a scholarly estimate for composition, not the 1916 edition date or the date of any digital object.
Retained as an attributed project estimate rather than collapsed into an exact date.

The broad English cultural origin does not prove a specific place of composition. Southwestern England and Gloucestershire describe scholarly dialect or witness-localization claims that remain uncertain for the abstract work.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The surrounding codex contains French and Latin works, Napier's apparatus is modern English, and the closing formula is Latin; none makes the cataloged poem as a whole multilingual.

form_statements.description: A 538-line narrative in rhyming couplets, mixing predominantly alexandrines with septenaries and some four-stress lines; some alliteration is present.

genre_statements.label: Romance-inflected biblical poetry
notes: This records formal and cultural participation in romance, not a claim that the poem is simply a secular romance.

subject_statements.label: Providence, fate, and free will
notes: The poem repeatedly attributes outcomes to God's will, though modern accounts differ on how overtly didactic the work is.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English poem retelling the Joseph cycle with Jacob as a persistent emotional center. Joseph's brothers sell him into Egypt; he rises at Pharaoh's court through dream interpretation, tests the brothers during famine, reveals himself, and reunites the family. The poem reshapes Genesis with vernacular, courtly, household, and romance-inflected details rather than translating the Vulgate mechanically.

Bodley 652 preserves the secure text on fols. 1r–10v, but one leaf has been cut out between fols. 6 and 7, so the extant 538-line poem is incomplete. A difficult, erased or reused text on fol. 3r of the fifteenth-century Delamere Chaucer (Takamiya MS 32) has been proposed as a related Jacob and Joseph fragment, but its exact textual relationship remains unresolved and it is not counted as a secure second witness here.

extent.description: One continuous extant poem of 538 L elements in the repository encoding and 538 printed lines in Napier's edition. Twenty manuscript-folio milestones mark fols. 1r–10v; one missing leaf between fols. 6 and 7 means this is extant rather than planned or original extent. The XML has no LG elements, so no stanza or verse-group count is asserted.

content_parts.label: Iacob and Iosep
content_parts.summary: The continuous verse narrative from its address to listeners through Jacob's reunion with Joseph, followed by the Latin explicit.
notes: The missing leaf is a physical lacuna, not a separately modeled content part. Napier's introduction, notes, glossary, and facsimile plates are edition apparatus outside the medieval work boundary.

related_works.title: Genesis 37–47 (Jacob and Joseph cycle)
notes: Genesis is the secure ultimate narrative source, but the poem also reflects medieval French, vernacular-biblical, iconographic, and romance traditions; no single immediate textual exemplar has been established.

related_works.title: Cursor Mundi (Joseph narrative)
notes: Shared narrative details and a complex relationship do not establish direct copying in either direction.

evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY DIV1 ACB1675.0001.001:3; lines 1–538; NOTE1 and MILESTONE elements; TRAILER
evidence.summary: Supplies the encoded poem, title page, U-M identifier, Napier source statement, OCR declaration, line and folio structure, missing-leaf note, manuscript-reading notes, and explicit.

evidence.citation: Element and content inspection of repository acb1675.xml.
evidence.locator: DLPSTEXTCLASS/TEXT and descendant DIV1, HEAD, L, LG, NOTE1, P, PB, and MILESTONE elements
evidence.summary: Counts 4 DIV1 elements, 1 HEAD, 538 L, no LG, 15 NOTE1, 7 P, 78 PB, and 20 folio MILESTONE elements. The 32 front, 22 body, and 24 back PB elements show that page images cover the edition while OCR text omits most front and back matter.

evidence.citation: Arthur S. Napier, ed., Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1916), Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. vii–xxxii; text pp. 1–18; notes pp. 19–23; glossary pp. 24–41; double facsimile
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for the title, Napier's role and independent transcription, Bodley 652 folio range and composite context, missing leaf, dating and dialect argument, mixed meter, editorial method, comparison with Heuser and other biblical traditions, and the two manuscript facsimile plates.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, ‘Joseph and Jacob’ text details.
evidence.summary: Provides title variants, scripture-paraphrase and ballad classifications, tentative 1260–1290 composition range, uncertain perhaps-Southern original dialect, couplet form, DIMEV/NIMEV identifiers, and Bodley 652 fols. 1r–10v as the secure witness.
notes: Its displayed 583 line count conflicts with Napier, Hume, ARLIMA, and the 538 XML L elements; this manifest uses the reproducible 538-line count and records the discrepancy implicitly through evidence attribution.

evidence.summary: Supplies the manuscript-title explicit, incipit, explicit, 538-line extent, Bodley 652 folio range, Heuser 1905 and Napier 1916 editions, and report of a 1998 reprint.

evidence.citation: Brian Murdoch, ‘The Middle English Iacob and Iosep and the Medieval Popular Bible,’ English Studies 98.8 (2017): 846–865.
evidence.summary: Analyzes the poem within the medieval popular Bible, qualifies proposed French and Cursor Mundi relationships, emphasizes Jacob's centrality, proposes didactic and baronial contexts, and reports that a fragment then in Tokyo may be related.

evidence.citation: Cathy Hume, ‘Iacob and Iosep: a happy tale of a knightly family,’ in Middle English Biblical Poetry: Romance, Audience and Tradition (Boydell & Brewer, 2021), pp. 31–58.
evidence.summary: Dates the poem probably to the second half of the thirteenth century, describes its 538 extant lines, unique Bodley witness and missing leaf, mixed couplet meters, biblical/ballad/romance contexts, and presents an interpretive household-chaplain and knightly-audience argument.

evidence.citation: ‘Old Testament Translation: Eighth to Fourteenth Centuries,’ Oxford Handbook of Medieval Literature in English.
evidence.summary: Characterizes the anonymous 538-line poem as a shorter Old Testament composition blending Vulgate narrative with romance and ballad traditions, largely retelling Genesis 37–47 with vernacular changes.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for acb1675, Bodley 652 catalog and IIIF evidence.
evidence.summary: Binds the work title, witness shelfmark, date/place attribution, material context, exact institutional catalog route, selected public image, and image terms to the lineage record.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for acb1675, Takamiya MS 32 candidate-fragment research.
evidence.summary: Binds the unresolved candidate fragment to Hume's conference-paper record, Murdoch's qualified report, the exact Yale catalog/IIIF object, and visual inspection of fol. 3r without treating it as a secure parallel witness.

open_questions.question: Is the difficult text on fol. 3r of Beinecke Takamiya MS 32 a second, reworked, or otherwise related witness of Iacob and Iosep, and what exact passage and transmission relationship does it preserve?
next_steps: Consult Cathy Hume's 2021 paper and any subsequent publication or catalog revision.
Obtain a specialist transcription and multispectral or enhanced images of fol. 3r, then collate it against Bodley 652 and Napier's text.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's original dialect and place of composition be distinguished from the language and production locality of Bodley 652?
next_steps: Review LAEME/LALME profiles and current scholarship on the Bodley 652 scribe and rhyme language.
Retain broad England cultural origin until composition-locality evidence is independent of the surviving witness.

open_questions.question: What can be established about the anonymous poet and intended audience beyond competing minstrel, clerically trained poet, household chaplain, baronial, and knightly hypotheses?
next_steps: Compare Murdoch's and Hume's arguments with the manuscript's codicological context and contemporary performance evidence.
Do not convert interpretive social profiles into known creator identities or certain audience metadata.

open_questions.question: What narrative material occupied the lost leaf between Bodley 652 fols. 6v and 7r, and which biblical, French, iconographic, or vernacular sources best explain the poem's non-Vulgate details?
next_steps: Compare the lacuna and distinctive episodes with the relevant Genesis, French Bible, Cursor Mundi, and iconographic traditions.
Keep possible source relationships qualified rather than forcing a single direct exemplar.

notes: Napier is editor and transcriber of the immediate print source; the inherited XML AUTHOR label must not make him author of the medieval poem.
The 538-line extent is extant and encoding-specific. One manuscript leaf is missing, and PCMEP's displayed 583 count is not adopted without a reproducible explanation.
Bodley 652 is a composite codex with adjacent French and Latin works. Those codex languages, dates, and subjects are not assigned to Iacob and Iosep as a whole.
The possible Takamiya fragment remains outside the secure witness count pending textual verification.
